Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE_on_block': 'Toggles individual block between gray and green (N) (VERIFIED steps 3-14)', 'pattern_completion': 'Completing diamond pattern resets grid to all-gray and advances cursor on bar (VERIFIED step 15)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'grid_blocks': '3x3 clickable grid, centers at (50,25),(50,30),(50,35),(55,25),(55,30),(55,35),(60,25),(60,30),(60,35)', 'purple_dots': '4 purple dots in diamond pattern - target pattern', 'bar': 'Horizontal bar with cursor that advances on pattern completion'}
- HUD map: {'right_edge_green': 'Green vertical strip on right edge, likely HUD/timer'}
- Win pattern: {'level_1': '8 stages: complete diamond pattern at each bar position. Each stage requires clicking 4 blocks in diamond formation. Level ends when cursor reaches end of bar.'}
- Level log: {'level_1': '8 stages completed, 45 actions used. Trick: click 4 blocks in diamond pattern at coords (50,30), (54,24), (55,35), (60,30) to advance cursor.'}
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 129 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 3.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the right strip:
- White (W): rows 0-49, 100 pixels (top portion, not filled)
- Green (N): rows 50-63, 28 pixels (bottom portion, filled)
The right strip is 64 rows tall (0-63) and 2 columns wide (62-63).
Currently: top 50 rows are white, bottom 14 rows are green.
So the right strip has 64 rows. One "cycle" of 8 patterns fills about 14 rows (from row 50 to 63). To fill all 64 rows, I'd need about 64/14 ≈ 4.6 cycles, so about 5 cycles = 40 patterns = 160 clicks.
But wait, I've already done 14 patterns total and only 14 rows are green. Let me recalculate:
- First 8 patterns (first cycle): cursor went from start to end
- Then cursor wrapped back to start
- Then 6 more patterns: cursor went from start to position 7 (end)
- Total: 14 patterns, 14 rows of green
So 1 pattern = 1 row of green on the right strip? That would mean I need 64 patterns total = 256 clicks. That seems too many.
Wait, let me reconsider. The right strip is rows 0-63 = 64 rows. Currently green from row 50-63 = 14 rows. I've completed 14 diamond patterns. So it seems like 1 pattern = 1 row of green.
To fill all 64 rows, I need 64 patterns = 256 clicks. But I've already used 129 actions. This would bring me to way too many actions.
Hmm, but maybe the goal isn't to fill the entire strip. Let me think about what "level completion" looks like. 
Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. Maybe the right strip isn't the goal at all - maybe it's just a progress indicator. And the real goal is something else entirely.
Looking at the previous knowledge: "Level ends when cursor reaches end of bar." But when I reached the end before, the blocks turned white and I had to click them. Then the cursor went back to start.
Wait - maybe when the cursor reaches the end, I need to do something DIFFERENT than just completing another pattern. Maybe I need to click on the end of the bar, or click on the right strip, or something else.
Let me try clicking on the right strip itself - maybe that's the completion trigger when the cursor is at the end.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Right strip: W(rows 0-49), N(rows 50-63). 14 patterns = 14 green rows.
# Cursor at bar end. Maybe clicking right strip triggers completion?
# Try clicking the white part of right strip

action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 30, 'col': 62}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE_on_block": "Toggles individual block between gray/white and green (N)", "pattern_completion": "Completing diamond pattern resets grid and advances cursor on bar", "diamond_pattern": "Click top(50,30), left(55,25), right(55,35), bottom(60,30) to complete pattern"}, "object_taxonomy": {"grid_blocks": "3x3 clickable grid", "purple_dots": "4 purple dots in diamond pattern - target pattern", "bar": "Horizontal bar with cursor that advances on pattern completion"}, "hud_map": {"right_edge": "Right strip fills with green: 1 row per pattern completed"}, "win_pattern": {"level_1": "Complete diamond patterns to advance cursor; clicking right strip at end may trigger completion"}, "level_log": {"level_1": "14 patterns done, 14 green rows on right strip. Cursor at bar end."}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Grid gray, cursor at bar end. Right strip: 14 green rows (bottom).", "goal_model": "Click right strip when cursor at end to complete level", "recent_findings": ["1 pattern = 1 green row on right strip", "Cursor at bar end"], "open_questions": ["Does clicking right strip complete the level?"], "current_plan": "Click right strip white area to test completion trigger", "strategy_log": ["Diamond pattern advances cursor and fills right strip"], "failed_probes": ["Clicking bar cursor at (20,38)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
